{"query":"Live Nation total treble damages calculation Ticketmaster antitrust April 2026 verdict","model":"mako","response_time_ms":1589,"verdict":null,"extra_info":null,"results":[{"url":"https://www.npr.org/2026/04/15/nx-s1-5786715/live-nation-ticketmaster-antitrust-verdict-monopoly","title":"Jury finds that Live Nation and Ticketmaster acted as a monopoly : NPR","content":"On Wednesday, a federal jury in Manhattan found that Live Nation and its subsidiary, Ticketmaster, operated as a monopoly that harmed consumers and overcharged ticket buyers.
The decision is a victory for 33 states and the District of Columbia, which accused Live Nation of unfairly controlling too many aspects of the live entertainment industry at the expense of venues, artists and fans after the company settled an earlier antitrust suit with the U.S.
In a statement issued after the verdict on Wednesday, Live Nation pointed to several outstanding motions still left for a judge to decide, including the potential dismissal of expert testimony cited by the jury in its verdict.
Pending motions will determine whether the liability and damages rulings stand," the statement reads.
Pearl Jam, Taylor Swift, The Cure and Olivia Dean are among the artists who have criticized how Ticketmaster has handled concert sales.
District Judge Arun Subramanian ordered both parties to meet with each other and "the United States" to propose a schedule for the next steps, according to the Associated Press."},{"url":"https://www.wtoc.com/2026/04/15/jury-finds-that-ticketmaster-live-nation-had-an-anticompetitive-monopoly-over-big-concert-venues/","title":"Jury finds that Ticketmaster and Live Nation had an anticompetitive monopoly over big concert venues","content":"Live Nation CEO Michael Rapino testified, answering questions about matters including the company’s Taylor Swift ticket debacle in 2022.
In its statement, Live Nation said the jury’s award of $1.72 per ticket applied to “a limited number of tickets” sold at 257 venues and representing about 20% of total tickets sold.
The company estimated the aggregate single damages figure would be below $150 million, though it would be trebled.
New York Attorney General Letitia James called the verdict “a landmark victory.”"},{"url":"https://blogs.duanemorris.com/antitrustlaw/2026/04/15/states-win-antitrust-case-against-live-nation-lessons-from-a-landmark-antitrust-case/","title":"States Win Antitrust Case Against Live Nation: Lessons from a Landmark Antitrust Case – Antitrust Law Blog","content":"On April 15, 2026, a federal jury found that Live Nation Entertainment and its Ticketmaster subsidiary violated federal and state antitrust laws.
Even when federal authorities settle, states may pursue more expansive relief."},{"url":"https://time.com/article/2026/04/16/live-nation-federal-antitrust-verdict-explainer/","title":"How Live Nation Verdict May Affect Ticket Prices","content":"After weeks of trials that began on March 2, a New York federal jury on Wednesday found that the company was operating an illegal monopoly over large entertainment venues.
The jury also found that Ticketmaster overcharged $1.72 per ticket in 21 states and in D.C. because of Live Nation’s anticompetitive practices.
The company also claimed that it should pay no more than $450 million for damages."},{"url":"https://www.businessinsider.com/live-nation-ticketmaster-verdict-antitrust-trial-illegal-monopoly-2026-4","title":"Live Nation Found Liable for Holding Monopoly in Antitrust Trial - Business Insider","content":"Ticketmaster parent company Live Nation holds an illegal monopoly over the concert and live event industry, a Manhattan federal jury found on Wednesday.
The entertainment giant was found liable for violating antitrust laws following a roughly six-week civil trial.
Jurors determined that Live Nation's monopoly led to fans being overcharged $1.72 per ticket sold through Ticketmaster from May 2020 to 2024.
The plaintiffs have argued that Live Nation controls 78% of the large amphitheaters used by artists and, through Ticketmaster, 86% of primary ticketing at major concert venues — meaning the initial sale of tickets.
"This is a historic and resounding victory for artists, fans, and the venues that support them," California Attorney General Rob Bonta said after the verdict was reached."}]}

tavily.raw: {"query":"Live Nation total treble damages calculation Ticketmaster antitrust April 2026 verdict","follow_up_questions":null,"answer":null,"images":[],"results":[{"url":"https://blogs.duanemorris.com/antitrustlaw/2026/04/15/states-win-antitrust-case-against-live-nation-lessons-from-a-landmark-antitrust-case","title":"States Win Antitrust Case Against Live Nation: Lessons from a Landmark Antitrust Case – Antitrust Law Blog","content":"# States Win Antitrust Case Against Live Nation: Lessons from a Landmark Antitrust Case. On April 15, 2026, a federal jury found that Live Nation Entertainment and its Ticketmaster subsidiary violated federal and state antitrust laws. The verdict holds critical lessons for any business relying on vertical integration, exclusive contracts, or data-driven strategies. The jury concluded that Live Nation unlawfully monopolized multiple live entertainment markets by leveraging its dominant position in concert promotion, venue ownership, and ticketing to foreclose competition. Artists seeking access to Live Nation’s 265-plus venues had to use Live Nation as promoter; venues wanting Live Nation tours had to use Ticketmaster. The exclusive dealing claims targeted Ticketmaster’s long-term contracts—often 3 to 14 years and terminable only for cause—that locked venues into exclusive arrangements even when competitors offered better terms. The presiding judge criticized the settlement’s delayed disclosure as “absolute disrespect for the court.” States pressed forward, signaling willingness to pursue more aggressive remedies than federal enforcers—keeping structural relief, including Ticketmaster divestiture, on the table.","score":0.7272211,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.npr.org/2026/04/15/nx-s1-5786715/live-nation-ticketmaster-antitrust-verdict-monopoly","title":"Jury finds that Live Nation acted as a monopoly and ...","content":"**Jury finds that Live Nation and Ticketmaster acted as a monopoly** **An antitrust suit alleged that the company unfairly controlled too much of the live music industry at the expense of venues, artists and fans. Live Nation, which owns the ticketing company Ticketmaster and owns or operates hundreds of venues, was accused in the antitrust lawsuit of unfairly wielding its power over the live music industry. On Wednesday, a federal jury in Manhattan found that Live Nation and its subsidiary, Ticketmaster, operated as a monopoly that harmed consumers and overcharged ticket buyers. ### The Live Nation trial could reshape the music industry. The decision is a victory for 33 states and the District of Columbia, which accused Live Nation of unfairly controlling too many aspects of the live entertainment industry at the expense of venues, artists and fans after the company settled an earlier antitrust suit with the U.S. Department of Justice. The verdict has the potential to reshape the live music industry in the United States.","score":0.6972179,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.crowell.com/en/insights/client-alerts/after-the-verdict-navigating-the-live-nationticketmaster-antitrust-fallout","title":"After the Verdict: Navigating the Live Nation/Ticketmaster ...","content":"**The litigation is far from over, and the stakes remain high.** Pending Rule 50 and Rule 59 motions, Tunney Act review, a full remedy phase, and an all-but-certain appeal mean the final outcome will not be known for years — but the jury’s clean sweep on liability gives the states significant leverage, and the concert-focused market definition leaves the door open for a parallel wave of sports-ticketing litigation. The jury found the defendants liable for $1.72 for each primary concert ticket sold pursuant to the anticompetitive conduct.The trial opened March 2, 2026, before Judge Arun Subramanian in the Southern District of New York, as a case brought by the federal government and a coalition of states. Next up for the case: (1) a statutorily required Tunney Act review of the DOJ’s settlement; (2) defendants’ Rule 50 and Rule 59 motions; (3) determination by the Court of how many tickets are subject to the $1.72 damage award (before trebling as per the Clayton Act); and (4) a remedy phase where the Court will consider plaintiffs’ likely proposal to sever Ticketmaster from Live Nation.","score":0.68420124,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/live-nation-antitrust-verdict-what-happened-what-it-means-and-what-comes-next","title":"Live Nation and Ticketmaster lose antitrust trial","content":"# Live Nation and Ticketmaster lose antitrust trial: What happened, what it means, and what comes next. A federal jury on Wednesday (April 15) found that **Live Nation Entertainment** and its subsidiary **Ticketmaster** illegally monopolized the US ticketing and amphitheater markets. The result handed a victory to the coalition of 33 states and the District of Columbia that pressed the landmark antitrust case to trial after the concert giant reached a settlement with the US Department of Justice. The jury found in favor of the states on every claim after a five-week trial in Manhattan federal court, and determined that consumers had been overcharged on tickets. Here’s what happened, how we got here, and what comes next…. ###### **What the jury found**. On damages, the jury found that consumers were overcharged by **$1.72** per ticket for primary concert tickets at major concert venues across 22 states and the District of Columbia.","score":0.62000114,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.justice.gov/atr/case/us-and-plaintiff-states-v-live-nation-entertainment-inc-and-ticketmaster-llc","title":"Antitrust Division |  U.S. and Plaintiff States v.
